title: Coherence-Preserving Fluctuation Diagnostics for an Engineered Population-Inverted Qubit Otto Engine
authors: Gabriella G. Damas, Norton G. de Almeida, Gao Xianlong, G. D. de Moraes Neto
abstract: Finite-time quantum thermal machines require diagnostics beyond average work and efficiency, because microscopic engines operate in regimes where fluctuations, incomplete thermalization, and coherence are equally important. Here we develop a measurement-backaction-free (coherence-preserving) fluctuation diagnostic for an engineered qubit Otto engine coupled to an actively maintained population-inverted hot channel. The engine is analyzed using a dynamic Bayesian network (DBN) reconstruction of the unmeasured coherent cycle, yielding work, heat, power, and normalized efficiency-proxy fluctuations without imposing the projective dephasing inherent in two-point energy measurements. The inverted channel is treated as an active reduced-model resource; accordingly, all reported power and efficiency enhancements represent gross working-medium advantages, not net device efficiencies. In the full-thermalization limit, population inversion enhances extracted work and output power while opening a stability sector with markedly reduced relative power fluctuations. When finite-duration isochores are implemented, this gross enhancement reorganizes into a structured operating landscape with distinct high-power, high-efficiency, and low-relative-noise sectors, whose boundaries are governed by the competing timescales of nonadiabatic driving and thermalization rates. A direct comparison reveals that DBN and conventional two-point measurement predictions diverge precisely in coherence-rich regimes, identifying where a backaction-free reconstruction is essential. A coherence-sensitive analysis further shows that the positive-temperature reference operates optimally in an almost decohered region, whereas the inverted high-efficiency branch remains aligned with the dominant post-hot-bath coherence ridge. These results provide a reduced-model benchmarking framework for engineered qubit thermal machines.
summary: This paper introduces a Dynamic Bayesian Network method to study quantum Otto engines without the dephasing caused by traditional measurements. It demonstrates that using a population-inverted hot reservoir significantly boosts power and work extraction while providing a more stable operating regime. The study highlights how coherence-preserving diagnostics are essential for accurately characterizing engines in highly coherent regimes.
main_problem: Developing a diagnostic framework for quantum thermal machines that accounts for fluctuations and coherence without the measurement-induced dephasing caused by conventional two-point energy measurements.
main_result: The Dynamic Bayesian Network (DBN) reconstruction accurately captures the coherent dynamics of a population-inverted qubit Otto engine, revealing that population inversion enhances work and power while creating a stable, low-noise operating sector.
method: A Dynamic Bayesian Network (DBN) reconstruction protocol is used to infer energy histories and fluctuation statistics, which is then compared against the standard Two-Point Measurement (TPM) scheme.
model_or_system: A single qubit undergoing a quantum Otto cycle (compression, hot isochore, expansion, cold isochore) coupled to a cold reservoir and an engineered, population-inverted hot channel. The dynamics are modeled using a Markovian master equation with non-commuting Hamiltonians.
key_observables: Work, heat, power, efficiency (regularized efficiency proxy), relative power fluctuations, and relative-entropy coherence.
important_parameters: Population inversion levels, driving time, isochore duration, energy gaps, and the ratio of upward to downward transition rates.
main_assumptions: The study uses a reduced-model approach that does not account for the work cost of maintaining the population inversion; it also assumes a Markovian, weak-coupling regime.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the Otto cycle schematic and DBN logic; Figure 2 compares performance landscapes (work, power, efficiency) for inverted vs. positive-temperature regimes; Figure 4 illustrates finite-time effects; Figure 7 validates the DBN via information-theoretic monotonicity bounds.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of measurement backaction, defines the DBN reconstruction method, compares it to TPM, analyzes the performance of the inverted engine in both full-thermalization and finite-time regimes, and concludes with a validation of the DBN framework using information-theoretic bounds.
why_interesting: It provides a novel, backaction-free method for studying the thermodynamics of coherent quantum systems, specifically showing how engineered reservoirs (like population inversion) can fundamentally alter the stability and efficiency landscape of quantum heat engines.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Schematic of the finite-time qubit Otto cycle considered in this work. The cycle consists of a compres- sion stroke, a hot isochore, an expansion stroke, and a cold isochore. During the hot isochore the engine absorbs heat from the hot reservoir, Qh > 0, while during the cold iso- chore it releases heat to the cold reservoir, Qc < 0. (b) Dy- namic Bayesian network representation of the five-corner cy- cle. The lower chain represents the sequence of corner-state variables along the cycle, while the upper nodes represent the inferred corner-energy outcomes ϵi. The horizontal arrows de- scribe the temporal propagation of the corner-state variables through the cycle, whereas the...

FIG. 2. Comparative fluctuation landscape in the ideal-reset regime. The top row corresponds to the population-inverted hot channel, equivalently the effective negative-temperature regime (Th < 0, ph e > 1/2), while the bottom row corresponds to the positive-temperature reference case (Th > 0, ph e < 1/2). The columns show the efficiency ηreg, mean work ⟨W⟩, output power P, and relative power fluctuation ϕP in the population plane (pc e, ph e), all evaluated at the driving time τ ⋆ drive that maximizes the output power for each population pair. The dashed curve in the inverted-regime work panel marks Wfric = 0. White regions indicate points outside the engine regime, namely parameter...

FIG. 3. Finite-time reduced population-inverted engine in the (τdrive, τiso) plane. Heatmaps show the mean work ⟨W⟩, efficiency ηreg, output power P, and relative power fluctuation ϕP = σ(P)/|⟨P⟩|. White regions correspond to points outside the engine regime. The finite-time landscape separates into distinct operational sectors: a short-time high-power sector, a narrow high- efficiency but strongly fluctuating sector, and a long-time low-relative-noise sector approaching the reset-like regime. The maps therefore provide design diagnostics rather than a single universal optimum.

FIG. 4. Finite-time operating landscape for the positive-temperature reference case in the (τdrive, τiso) plane, showing the extracted work ⟨W⟩, efficiency ηreg, output power P, and relative power fluctuation ϕP = σ(P)/|⟨P⟩|. This figure is included as an appendix reference baseline for comparison with the population-inverted operating landscape discussed in the main text and with the coherence-sensitive analysis. The white region corresponds to points outside the engine regime.

FIG. 5. Finite-time thermodynamic and coherence landscapes in the (τdrive, τiso) plane. The top row corresponds to the effective negative-temperature regime, while the bottom row shows the positive-temperature reference case. The columns display the output power P, the efficiency ηreg, and the relative-entropy coherence after the hot bath, Crel(ρ3; Hh). White regions indicate points outside the engine regime. In each row, the markers indicate the maximum-power point, the maximum-efficiency point, the maximum-coherence point, and the minimum-coherence point.
